figures.title: Stressed skin action in a flat-roof building
figures.description: 3D structural diagram showing (a) Sheeting, (b) Shear field in sheeting, (c) Flange forces in edge members.

figures.title: Stressed skin action in a pitched roof building
figures.description: 3D structural diagram showing (a) Sheeting, (b) Flange forces in edge members, (c) Shear field in sheeting, (d) Gable tie required to resist forces in roof sheeting.
labels: (d) Gable tie required to resist forces in roof sheeting

figures.title: Arrangement of an individual panel
figures.description: 3D view of a roof panel showing (a) Rafter, (b) Purlin, (c) Shear connector, (d) Sheet-to-shear connector fastener, (e) Purlin, (f) Sheet-to-purlin fastener, (g) Seam fastener. Shows dimensions a, b, and forces F, Fa/b.

figures.title: Location of seam fasteners
figures.description: 3D view of liner trays showing seam fasteners located at spacing e_s and distance e_u from the wide flange. Shows dimensions b_f, h.

figures.title: Examples of appropriate test accessories
figures.description: 3D and cross-sectional views of profiled sheeting showing test accessories: (a) Rivet or screw, (b) Transverse tie (metal strip), (c) Timber blocks.

figures.title: Test set-up for single span tests
figures.description: Diagrams showing single span test setups: a) Uniformly distributed loading and an example of alternative equivalent line loads (F/2 at L/4 and 3L/4), b) Distributed loading applied by an airbag, c) Example of support arrangements for preventing distortion, d) Example of method of applying a line load.
labels: a) Uniformly distributed loading and an example of alternative equivalent line loads
b) Distributed loading applied by an airbag (alternatively by a vacuum test rig)
c) Example of support arrangements for preventing distortion

figures.title: Test setup for double span tests
figures.description: Diagram showing a double span test setup with uniformly distributed loading over two spans of length L.

figures.title: Examples of suitable arrangements of alternative line loads
figures.description: Shows two simply supported beams with multiple point loads F applied. Top beam has 6 loads F spaced at L/4, with end distances L/8. Bottom beam has 4 loads F spaced at 0.35L and 0.525L, with end distances 0.125L.

figures.title: Test set-up for internal support test
figures.description: Diagrams showing internal support test setups: a) Internal support under gravity loading, b) Internal support under uplift loading, c) Internal support with loading applied to tension flange. Shows forces F, support width b_B, and depth h.
labels: a) Internal support under gravity loading
b) Internal support under uplift loading
c) Internal support with loading applied to tension flange

figures.title: Test set-up for end support tests
figures.description: Shows a beam with an end support and an internal support. Load F applied at distance ≈ L/3 from end support. Dimensions u, b_A ≥ h, 300 mm, ≥ 3h, L. Reaction R_A. Key: b_A = support length, u = length from internal edge of end support to end of sheet.
labels: u = length from internal edge of end support to end of sheet

figures.title: Test set-up for internal support tests
figures.description: Shows a beam with supports at A, C, E. Load F applied at C. Points B and D are located at distance e from supports A and E respectively. Total span is s.

figures.title: Relation between support moment M and support reaction R
figures.description: Graph plotting M on the y-axis and R on the x-axis. Shows (a) test results for different test spans s as points, and (b) linear interpolation as lines connecting the points.

figures.title: Relation between load F and net deflection δ

figures.title: Derivation of moment-rotation (M - θ) characteristic
figures.description: Graph plotting M on the y-axis and θ on the x-axis. Shows curves for tests 1 and 2, a mean curve M_mean, a design value curve M_d, and a curve for 0,9 M_mean.
labels: M_mean = mean value, M_d = design value

figures.title: Experimental determination of spring stiffness K_A and K_B
figures.description: Shows two alternatives for test setups. Alternative 1 shows a profile (c) attached to sheeting (a) with fastener (b), load (d) applied laterally, and clamped support (e). Dimensions b-a, b, ≥ 2b, ≥ 450 mm, h. Alternative 2 shows a profile (c) on timber blocks (f) with sheeting (a), fastener (b), load (d), and insulation (e). annexes.title: [informative] - Durability of fasteners
annexes.text:
(1) In Construction Classes I, II and III table B.1 may be applied.
NOTE: Fastener of steel without coating may be used in corrosion classification class C1.
A = Aluminium irrespective of surface finish
B = Un-coated steel sheet
C = Hot-dip zinc coated (Z275) or aluzink coated (AZ150) steel sheet
D = Hot-dip zinc coated steel sheet + coating of paint or plastics
E = Aluzink coated (AZ185) steel sheet
S = Stainless steel
X = Type of material recommended from the corrosion standpoint
(X) = Type of material recommended from the corrosion standpoint under the specified condition only
- = Type of material not recommended from the corrosion standpoint
a Refers to rivets only
b Refers to screws and nuts only
c Insulating washer, of material resistant to ageing, between sheeting and fastener
d Stainless steel EN 10 088
e Risk of discoloration.
f Always check with sheet supplier
(2) The environmental classification following EN-ISO 12944-2 is presented in table B.2.

annexes.title: [informative] - Cross section constants for thin-walled cross sections
[informative] - Mixed effective width/effective thickness method for outstand elements
annexes.text:
(1) This annex gives an alternative to the effective width method in 5.5.2 for outstand elements in compression. The effective area of the element is composed of the element thickness times an effective width be0 and an effective thickness teff times the rest of the element width bp . See Table D.1.
The slenderness parameter λp and reduction factor ρ is found in 5.5.2 for the buckling factor kσ in Table D.1.
The stress relation factor ψ in the buckling factor kσ may be based on the stress distribution for the gross cross section.
(2) The resistance of the section should be based on elastic stress distribution over the section.
